title: When a friend becomes your boss
summary: Analyse how a workplace promotion that puts one friend in charge of another reshapes the friendship, and whether the two roles can survive together.
topic: How authority changes a friendship at work
prompt: Write a 450–600-word analytical essay on what happens to a friendship when one friend is promoted to manage the other. Analyse the specific pressures the new authority creates, and judge whether equality and hierarchy can coexist. Use two concrete examples and concede one objection. Use cleft sentences, passive voice, and hedging.
hintStarters: What changes the morning after the promotion is not the work but…
Granted, plenty of friendships have survived the move from equal to…
It is the small asymmetries — who now signs off whose leave — that…
The friendship does not end so much as it is asked to…

hintVocab.meaningVi: thứ bậc / hệ thống cấp bậc
sự mất cân bằng quyền lực
chịu sự quản lý của / báo cáo cho
đánh giá năng lực làm việc
buộc ai đó chịu trách nhiệm
giữ cho mọi thứ công bằng
chuỗi mệnh lệnh / hệ thống cấp bậc
đảm nhận vai trò lớn hơn

sampleText:
On a Friday, Mei and Daniel were colleagues who complained about the same manager over lunch. On Monday, Mei was that manager. Nothing about the office had changed — the desks, the deadlines, the bad coffee were all the same — and yet the friendship they had built across two years of shared grievances now had a new weight pressing on it.

What changes the morning after the promotion is not the work but the channel through which everything must now travel. Before, a frustration could be aired freely; now, anything Daniel says to Mei is also being said to his boss. A complaint that once cost nothing might be read as insubordination, or worse, ignored. It is the small asymmetries — who now signs off whose leave, who decides whose project gets resourced — that quietly rearrange a relationship built on the assumption that neither party held power over the other. The candour that friendship runs on is precisely what the new arrangement makes risky.

Two pressures appear almost at once. The first falls on the manager. If Mei goes easy on Daniel, the rest of the team will sense favouritism, and her authority will be undermined; if she overcorrects, holding him to a harsher standard than anyone else, the friend who trusted her will feel betrayed. Either way, every ordinary decision is now read through the lens of their history. The second pressure falls on the friend. Daniel must decide what can still be confided and what must be withheld, and that calculation, repeated daily, slowly drains a friendship of its ease.

Granted, plenty of friendships have survived the move from equal to manager, and to claim otherwise would be too neat. Where both people name the change openly — where Mei says, in effect, 'I will sometimes have to be your manager first' — the relationship can be renegotiated rather than quietly strained. Honesty about the new asymmetry tends to protect a friendship better than pretending it has not occurred. The objection is fair: authority need not be fatal to friendship.

Even so, what survives is rarely the same friendship. It is one thing to stay close; it is another to stay equal, and these are routinely confused. A friend who must also write your performance review cannot fully be the person you complain to about your performance review. Something is necessarily held back, and the relationship, while it may remain warm, is no longer conducted between equals. The two roles can coexist, but only if both people accept that the friendship has been moved onto different ground.

What the promotion really tests, then, is not loyalty but flexibility — the willingness of two people to let a relationship change shape rather than insisting it stay exactly as it was. Mei and Daniel can remain friends; what they cannot do is pretend that one of them does not now sign the other's leave requests. The friendship does not end so much as it is asked to grow a hierarchy it never had, and whether it can do that gracefully is decided not on the day of the promotion but in the hundred small decisions that follow.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: It-cleft to locate the source of strain
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'It is the small asymmetries — who now signs off whose leave — that quietly rearrange a relationship' dùng it-cleft để làm nổi bật nguyên nhân thực sự (những bất đối xứng nhỏ) thay vì những thay đổi lớn dễ thấy. Mệnh đề chèn giữa hai dấu gạch ngang minh họa cụ thể.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'It is the small asymmetries — who now signs off whose leave — that quietly rearrange a relationship' uses an it-cleft to spotlight the real cause (small asymmetries) rather than the obvious big changes. The dashed insertion gives a concrete illustration.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Passive to keep focus on the friendship, not the actor
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'the relationship can be renegotiated', 'the friendship has been moved onto different ground', và 'it is asked to grow a hierarchy' dùng bị động để giữ trọng tâm vào mối quan hệ như một thực thể bị tác động, khi ai tác động ít quan trọng hơn điều gì xảy ra với nó.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'the relationship can be renegotiated', 'the friendship has been moved onto different ground', and 'it is asked to grow a hierarchy' use the passive to keep focus on the relationship as the thing acted upon, when who acts matters less than what happens to it.

sampleTranslationVi:
Vào một ngày thứ Sáu, Mei và Daniel là đồng nghiệp cùng than phiền về một người quản lý trong bữa trưa. Đến thứ Hai, Mei chính là người quản lý đó. Chẳng có gì ở văn phòng thay đổi — những chiếc bàn, các hạn chót, cà phê dở đều y như cũ — vậy mà tình bạn họ đã xây dựng qua hai năm cùng chung những bất bình giờ đây có một sức nặng mới đè lên nó.

Cái thay đổi vào buổi sáng sau khi thăng chức không phải là công việc mà là kênh mà giờ đây mọi thứ phải đi qua. Trước đây, một nỗi bực dọc có thể được bày tỏ thoải mái; giờ đây, bất cứ điều gì Daniel nói với Mei cũng đang được nói với sếp của anh ấy. Một lời than phiền từng chẳng tốn gì giờ có thể bị hiểu là sự bất tuân, hoặc tệ hơn, bị phớt lờ. Chính những sự bất đối xứng nhỏ — ai giờ phê duyệt phép của ai, ai quyết định dự án của ai được cấp nguồn lực — mới lặng lẽ sắp xếp lại một mối quan hệ được xây dựng trên giả định rằng không bên nào nắm quyền lực với bên kia. Sự thẳng thắn mà tình bạn vận hành nhờ vào nó chính là điều mà sự sắp xếp mới khiến trở nên rủi ro.

Hai áp lực xuất hiện gần như cùng lúc. Áp lực thứ nhất đè lên người quản lý. Nếu Mei nhẹ tay với Daniel, phần còn lại của nhóm sẽ cảm nhận được sự thiên vị, và quyền lực của cô ấy sẽ bị xói mòn; nếu cô bù trừ quá mức, đòi hỏi anh theo một tiêu chuẩn khắt khe hơn bất kỳ ai khác, thì người bạn từng tin tưởng cô ấy sẽ cảm thấy bị phản bội. Dù theo cách nào, mỗi quyết định bình thường giờ đây đều được nhìn qua lăng kính của lịch sử giữa họ. Áp lực thứ hai đè lên người bạn. Daniel phải quyết định điều gì còn có thể tâm sự và điều gì phải giữ lại, và sự tính toán đó, lặp lại mỗi ngày, dần dần rút cạn sự thoải mái của một tình bạn.

Công nhận, nhiều tình bạn đã sống sót qua bước chuyển từ ngang bằng sang quản lý, và khẳng định điều ngược lại sẽ quá gọn ghẽ. Khi cả hai người gọi tên sự thay đổi một cách cởi mở — khi Mei nói, về thực chất, 'Đôi khi tôi sẽ phải làm quản lý của bạn trước đã' — mối quan hệ có thể được thương lượng lại thay vì lặng lẽ căng thẳng. Sự trung thực về sự bất đối xứng mới có xu hướng bảo vệ một tình bạn tốt hơn là giả vờ rằng nó chưa xảy ra. Phản đối này là công bằng: quyền lực không nhất thiết là chí mạng với tình bạn.

Dẫu vậy, cái sống sót hiếm khi là tình bạn cũ. Giữ được sự gần gũi là một chuyện; giữ được sự ngang bằng lại là chuyện khác, và hai điều này thường bị lẫn lộn. Một người bạn lại còn phải viết bản đánh giá năng lực của bạn thì không thể hoàn toàn là người mà bạn than phiền về bản đánh giá đó. Một thứ gì đó tất yếu bị giữ lại, và mối quan hệ, dù có thể vẫn ấm áp, không còn được tiến hành giữa những người ngang bằng. Hai vai trò có thể cùng tồn tại, nhưng chỉ khi cả hai người chấp nhận rằng tình bạn đã được dời sang một mảnh đất khác.

Vậy thì, điều mà sự thăng chức thực sự thử thách không phải là lòng trung thành mà là sự linh hoạt — sự sẵn lòng của hai người để một mối quan hệ thay đổi hình dạng thay vì khăng khăng giữ nó y như cũ. Mei và Daniel có thể vẫn là bạn; điều họ không thể làm là giả vờ rằng một trong hai giờ đây không ký vào đơn xin nghỉ phép của người kia. Tình bạn không kết thúc cho bằng nó được yêu cầu phải mọc ra một thứ bậc mà nó chưa bao giờ có, và liệu nó có làm được điều đó một cách duyên dáng hay không được quyết định không phải vào ngày thăng chức mà trong cả trăm quyết định nhỏ theo sau.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a what-cleft to redefine what really changes?
options: The morning after the promotion changes what is not the work but a channel travelling.
What changes the morning after the promotion is not the work but the channel through which everything must now travel.
Changing the morning is what the promotion does to the work and the channel.
The channel is what changes the work after the morning promotion travels through.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'What changes … is not X but Y' is a what-cleft delaying the key contrast for emphasis.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'What … is not X but Y'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses an it-cleft to pinpoint the source of strain?
options: The small asymmetries quietly rearrange a relationship which is who signs off leave.
Quietly rearranging a relationship is the asymmetries small that signs off leave.
It is the small asymmetries — who now signs off whose leave — that quietly rearrange a relationship.
Who signs off leave is the relationship that small asymmetries quietly rearrange it.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'It is X … that …' is an it-cleft foregrounding the small asymmetries as the true cause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses the passive most naturally?
options: Both people the friendship must be moving onto different ground by them.
Moving the friendship onto different ground is what been done by people.
The friendship moving onto different ground was did by both people.
The friendship has been moved onto different ground.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'The friendship has been moved' is a clean passive keeping the relationship in subject position.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option uses 'has been + past participle' correctly?

options: A situation where one person holds more authority than another
An equal sharing of authority between two people
A failure of the electrical supply at work
A fair distribution of pay
mcQuestions.explanation: A 'power imbalance' is an uneven distribution of authority.
mcQuestions.hint: Think about one side having more power than the other.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option introduces the counter-argument most naturally?
options: Some people are just bad at being managers, full stop.
Granted, plenty of friendships have survived the move from equal to manager, and to claim otherwise would be too neat.
Obviously friendship and work should never ever mix at all.
Of course every promoted friend instantly becomes a tyrant.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted, …' concedes a real counter-point and warns against an over-tidy claim.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option fairly admits the other side has a case?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option hedges its claim appropriately?
options: Honesty about the new asymmetry always guarantees a friendship survives.
Honesty about the new asymmetry will never make any difference whatsoever.
Honesty about the new asymmetry tends to protect a friendship better than pretending it has not occurred.
Honesty about asymmetry the new tends protecting a friendship pretending occurred.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Tends to protect … better than' is a measured comparison, not an absolute promise.

options: Treating everyone exactly the same
Promoting people based on test scores
Refusing to manage your friends
Giving unfair preferential treatment to someone you like
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Favouritism' is unfair preference shown to a particular person.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of favouring one person over the rest.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence keeps a consistent analytical register?
options: Every ordinary decision is now read through the lens of their history.
Honestly it's super awkward and everyone just hates it when this happens.
Basically the friend gets thrown under the bus and that's that.
It's kind of a mess and nobody really knows what to do, you know?
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B uses precise abstract phrasing; the others slide into casual speech.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best pivots from concession back to the thesis?
options: In conclusion, friendship always wins.
Even so, what survives is rarely the same friendship.
On the contrary, nothing ever changes at all.
Anyway, let us drop that idea now.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even so, …' accepts the concession then advances a sharper claim.
mcQuestions.hint: Which phrase both accepts and qualifies the previous point?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to confide in' someone mean?
options: To give someone an order
To formally report someone to HR
To share private thoughts or feelings with someone you trust
To pay someone for their work
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Confide in' means to trust someone with personal information.
mcQuestions.hint: Think about sharing a secret with a trusted person.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses parallel structure to sharpen the contrast?
options: Staying close is one thing while to be equal another thing it is.
One thing close staying; equal another it is to stay being.
To stay close is a thing and equal staying is the other being.
It is one thing to stay close; it is another to stay equal.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'It is one thing to …; it is another to …' is a balanced parallel contrast.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option repeats the same frame twice?

mcQuestions.prompt: According to the essay, what does the promotion really test?
options: The willingness of both people to let the relationship change shape
The friends' loyalty to the company
Whether either friend deserves the job
How much the team likes the new manager
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay says it tests 'not loyalty but flexibility — the willingness … to let a relationship change shape.'
mcQuestions.hint: Reread the final paragraph's opening contrast.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to renegotiate' a relationship mean?
options: To end it permanently
To agree new terms for how it will work
To report it to a manager
To keep it exactly the same
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Renegotiate' means to settle on new arrangements.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option best states the essay's nuanced conclusion?
options: A promoted friend always destroys the friendship.
Friendship and authority are completely unrelated.
The two roles can coexist, but only if both accept the friendship has moved onto different ground.
The friends should simply stop speaking to each other.
mcQuestions.explanation: The conclusion allows coexistence on a condition, rather than declaring doom or denial.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the option with a condition attached ('only if').

options: To strengthen it openly
To formally record it
To share it equally
To weaken or erode it, often gradually
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Undermine' means to weaken something, often from below or indirectly.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of digging away the ground 'under' something.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses an appositive (dash insertion) correctly to add a concrete example?
options: The small asymmetries — who now signs off whose leave — quietly rearrange the relationship.
The small asymmetries who now signs off whose leave — quietly rearrange relationship the.
The small — asymmetries who signs off whose leave now — rearrange quietly relationship.
Quietly rearrange — the small asymmetries — relationship who signs off whose leave.
mcQuestions.explanation: The example sits between two dashes as an appositive, with the main clause grammatical without it.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option lets you remove the dashed part and still have a full sentence?

criticalThinkingQuestion: Have you ever had to give an instruction to a friend, or take one from one? What did you each hold back that you would have said as equals, and was the friendship better or worse for the silence?
